sentences.text: Yes, I would like to go back to my mother, because I'm keep on slipping, forgetting a few things.

I learned since from my sisters that my mother, having no children of her own, and the lady next door which were neighbor, she had about-- obviously grown up children from about 20 downwards, but she had a lot of children. 10 at least, if not more.
My mother helped her.
She took her a couple of her kids to carry on her-- her lap.
And we believe to this day, my brother and-- and my sisters, that she was strong enough if she didn't have these kids she would have been selected for work.
But they didn't stop to ask, are they your kids, or not.
So by having one, at least, if not two children in her arms, straight to the gas chambers.
And I-- I will always believe that until the day I die.

And talking about believing, I-- I still-- I think I mentioned it on the film before-- because my younger brother was taken off from my father, because he was after all four and a half years younger than me, I looked tall, big against him.
So they took him, and they said you're going back to your mother.
And I was selected for work.

And I was still, in my mind, if I lived to 120, I believe if he wouldn't have been with us, if he would have gone with my mother, probably I would have been chosen because I next-- against my father, I was very little because my father was six foot plus.
And that's my belief, and I'm sticking to it.
Right.
And your-- your younger brother was told he was going off to his mother.
But that wasn't actually what happened.
That was not-- of course, that was not the case.
Of course, we learned later.
I mean, OK, my father says.
See you later, or something to that effect.
Because you believed.
They said he is going back to his mother.
And that's what we believed, until obviously after the war, we learned all these terrible things which happened.
Yes.
Yeah.
Well, they were-- they-- we could see them, sort of.
They were in a different line.
Again, five in a line, sort of long, long queues.
They were going-- supposed to be going to the bathing, to the wash, and get ready for work, get ready, and you'll be-- you'll be taken where you have a hot meal and all the rest of it.
But they are kidding you all the time, and you believed.
Because we were simple people.
We came from villages.
I don't know how the rest-- the people who came from big towns and all that took it.
But we were-- just believed everything what people told us.
I mean, even in-- not only the Germans.
If there was some kapos and things, and they were saying like, you'll be taken.
You'll be-- you will be bathed, and you will be going to your own place.
And you'll be given a house and all the rest of it.
We know now what happened, don't we?
Oh.
Yeah.
In Buchenwald, no.
I saw some beatings, because occasionally there was-- people did try to run away, and they caught them.
And there was beatings before hanging.
I saw myself in Buchenwald beatings, 25 lashes.
And then the people, about six or seven, were hung in front of the whole-- everybody had to be on Appellplatz to watch it.
That's what's going to happen to you if you try it.
So--
Every day.
Every day.
Every morning you-- used-- you stood there to be counted before you went to work.
Every evening, you had to be counted before you went to your block, back to your block.
Not as far as I know.
There was nothing to wash.
You only had a pair of pajamas, striped clothes.
I don't-- don't remember even whether there was no underwear.
There was no paper.
There was a-- latrines where there could probably be 20 people at the time could go.
But I don't really collect.
I mean, what you did, you-- you used to-- if you're lucky, you found a piece of paper, or-- or mostly leaves.
You wiped yourself with leaves and all that.
And talking about leaves, many months later I would have loved to have had them leaves to eat, because I will come along later and tell you about Belsen.
That was out of this-- just unbelievable.
Yeah.
We got two meals a day, and the food was better the like-- than it was in Auschwitz-- in Auschwitz as far as I'm concerned.
But-- or I got used to it, one of the two.
Because in-- in the-- the week or 10 days I was there, I just couldn't touch it.
I couldn't eat it.
But gradually, you get used to everything.
And I knew that if I didn't eat it, I will have nothing.
We used to have a lump of-- a lump of brown-- black bread, used to get, which I understand was made-- half of it was mixed with sawdust.
And that's how you got on from day to day to day to day.
I didn't know whether it was Monday, Sunday, Wednesday, or Friday.
It was just another day to us.
[GERMAN], work, and all the rest of it.
I did get the-- yes, I believe so.
I believer I did get more than-- which really-- and the workers needed more, because they were out smashing stones and breaking-- breaking things.
And some were working in-- making war material, and all the rest of it.
But I-- I must say, I did not.
And I-- now I've-- I mean, at the time, I thought I was hard done by.
But since then-- obviously since the war, I've met a lot of people, and I've talked to a lot of friends.
And I found what I had comparing to what they were going through was chicken feed.
And it was not easy.
It was-- it was not easy because I was on my own.
I had no father, no family, no-- nobody to call on.
And I don't even remember.
You make friends with people, and then next day they-- they've transported them to somewhere else, and so on.
There was only no names, just numbers all the time.
I was then 15.
Nobody asked who you are.
They-- they just said 54990.
Come here.
No.
No.
No.
No.
They were-- we just talked.
We didn't-- we didn't call each other Yankel or Shloimo, whatever.
We-- I don't-- I don't remember.
We don't-- did not exchange first names.
It were just-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
What are you doing today?
I mean, I-- not what are you doing today.
I'm getting mixed up.
It's what I-- what I'm saying to-- now.
But it-- I-- it doesn't-- there was no friendship.
You couldn't make friends with anybody, because they were transporting people left, right, and center, for no reason at all.

Like I'll come to you and tell you later about what happened to me at Buchenwald, as I said, when they came at Christmas-- round about Christmas time and called up my number, and-- and took us off and sort of finished up going on this train.
For a-- I should imagine because of interference with bombing and all that, took anything from 10 to 14 hours till we arrived in this place, which is called, I learned later, Hanover.
And there, there was really heavy bombings.
And we were taken into this bunker.
Into this sort of-- what do you call it now?
Shelter.
Where thousands and thousands of people were rushing down.

Right out of the train, they were running down and-- and there was-- as I said, we-- we held on to the-- virtually there's two soldiers and an officer, and we held on to their greatcoats not to get lost.
We didn't even think about or dream about escaping or where-- where you going to go?
What are you going to do?
We were just like little [YIDDISH].
Just like little [YIDDISH].
Yeah.
So how long were you in Buchenwald altogether?
I was in Buchenwald, I reckon, about eight and a half to nine months, which after-- I learned afterwards comparing to some of my friends, had it quite easy, if I may say so.
Good going.
But my problem arose when I was taken from this-- in the morning, in the daylight.
We took a local train from Hanover to Belsen.
They-- they-- they came.
As I said, they came and called out the numbers.
I-- I don't know-- I thought obviously just me.
But eventually we finished up in a room, and there were 17 of us.
And they was from about nine, 10, to 17.
And we were taken on this train, as I was saying.
Private train.
Never saw a train like that before.
With seating, with windows, with-- you know, what the train where we went on was cattle trains, 150 people, no seats.
Sitting more or less in-- in each other's laps to make room.
I believe there was some straw.
And then sort of a year later, to be taken on a train like a civilian train.
I just didn't know what was happening.
I've never been on a-- a train like that before.
And it just took everything.
Go here, go there.
And we did.
And that's what we did.
Yeah.

On that train, as I said, I don't really quite know because we-- we left-- because of the interruption of the bombings, we-- I should imagine anything from eight, 10, 12, or even 14 hours, until we are arrived in this-- at night, nighttime, which I learned later was Hanover, near Celle, actually.
This place where we-- we were taken into-- when we arrived in Hanover-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now I'm getting myself mixed up.
It might have been Celle, the nearest big town to where eventually I finished up in, which was, as I said, Belsen.

But considering that we were only in the camps about 13 and 1/2 months, Hungarians, what we were at the time-- and I learned later that some of my friends, Polish, were in there three, four, five years.
It was lighter for me.
But it was the hardest 15 or 18 months of the war, because the Germans by then were being pushed back from the Russian front.
And they themselves were short of food, and all that.
So can you imagine how the inmates in these camps were starved of food?
I mean, I've learned since by reading that the Germans were allowed so many-- two and a half thousand calories a day.
The Poles one and a half thousand.
And the-- the Jews about 600, 700 calories a day.
And they didn't give you the-- this food because they wanted you to sort of be strong, be-- be-- be where you are.
They just slowly starve you to death.
Slowly starve you to death.
Yeah.
So it was.
You know, it's-- So--
--it's-- you sort of-- I shut my eyes and I shudder, and like think to myself.
Was I really there?
Did-- did it really happen?
You got to-- I got to pinch myself to wake up to it, what my eyes have seen, and what I've seen happening to people.
It's just-- I'm-- it's just shattering.
For-- for years afterwards, even when I got married, my wife used to have to wake me up in the night because I was dreaming and screaming out about what-- what I've seen, and what was going on.
For years later.
I just couldn't rest.
Just felt-- I felt-- I'll gave you an example.
When I was 54 years of age-- and my father died at 54-- for nearly a whole year, I was so low, I was so sad, I even grew a beard.
I felt guilty that I'm going on for 55, and he died at 54.
And it just things like that which happened since, and it's just a terror-- just terrible.
Just absolutely terrible.
I felt-- I felt as if I've-- guilty.
That here am I.
I'm still alive.
I'm living in England.
And-- and my father, by the way, died because he was caught exchanging his bread, what they gave him, for a cigarette.
And they caught him, and he got 25 lashes.
And my brother, they-- they met up in some camp or other.
My brother went up, and he said, leave him alone.
He's an old man.
Give him to me.
So they said, you want some too?
We'll give you some too.
And he never recovered from that.
A few days later, he passed away.
Your father?
Yeah.
Never recovered from that.
They didn't give you-- when I say 25 lashes, they didn't give you lashes.
It was with a pick axe handle, or with a-- or with a lump of wood.
It-- it's easy to say he got 25 lashes.
Don't forget that they were also very weak from hunger, and from thirst, and from overwork.
So it was touch and go.
After-- after that, it was nearly-- it was nearly finished him off.
They nearly finished him off.
Yeah.
That is what my brother told me many years later after the war, because of course I-- I didn't know.
I wasn't there.
And that's what he told me.
Went up and told them, leave him alone.
He's an old man.
Yeah.
So your brother got the beating as well?
No.
They-- no, he didn't.
He said, if you want some, we'll give you some as well.
And that's what happened in that respect.
But if I may go back to Celle or Hanover-- I don't know which.
I'm confused.
Sorry.
We were taken in the morning with a very-- I remember it was a horrible rainy day.
On a local train [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a transport of women to-- it finished up-- later I realized it was another camp called Belsen.
I was still in civilian clothes, dressed like a toff.
We arrived in Belsen.
And it-- a woman transport arrived at the same time, all looking very hungry, almost starving.
And the officer went up and reported special transport, what I took it to be.
And the guy at the gate said, no, no, no.
Let them go in with the rest of them, with the women.
He said, special transport.
Special delivery.
We were taken to a block-- was a ladies'-- a woman's block.
And they started with the young-- young-- younger ones.
They put them in tubs.
And these ladies, Jewish women, they were scrubbing and washing them.
First time I've seen soap since I left home.
And I was sort of starting being a little embarrassed because there was these women.
And after all, I was now 15 and 1/2 or so.
And I was sort of thinking about, you know, they going to wash me and all the rest of it, my private parts.
But before-- I needn't have worried-- before the last four or five were taken to be scrubbed and washed, a German came.
He called out our numbers, and he took us off to another part of the camp.
It wasn't-- and-- and for 30 years or more, I was thinking, what was the idea behind it?
Why, why, why, why, why?
I spoke to a friend of mine.
She was a nurse-- a-- a sister, in fact, she-- she is.
And I was telling her this very same story.
And suddenly, she woke me up and said, don't you know?
Don't you realize why they did it?
They were going to use you as rent boys.
Said, oh my god.
There was no other way-- there is no other thing they could have done all this to us.
On hindsight, that is only thing they did do what they did, make us sort of dressed in civilian clothes and taking special delivery and special this and special that.
And she must be 150% right, what she explained to me.
That was only 10 years ago.
And we were away on holiday, and I was telling her this story.
And there-- there's no other way it could have been.
They could-- for no other reason.
Yeah.
And that part-- as I said, we finished up being taken to another part of Belsen.
And once again, I was lucky in a way because there was a-- a Lageralteste number two who hadn't seen any children for five years.
And the first thing he said is if possible, second helping of soup for these boys.
There was only four or five of us.
And I swear to you as I'm sitting here, I did get occasionally second helping of water, whatever it was.
And I-- and there was a-- man come up to me and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And once or twice, I managed to spare a drop of soup for this person.
I don't know whether it was true or not.
I take it as yes.
Somebody comes and tells me there's a [GERMAN].
Have you got a spare bit of soup?
I did have, and I did give, once or twice, half a dozen times.
I can't even-- I can't tell you.
That was that.
But then Belsen was a camp, no gas chambers.
No work.
You just went there to die.
I-- I read since there was about 480 people together in Belsen.
When the British marched in, that was about half.
No.
I'm telling a lie.
There was 100,000 left.
By the end of the day, they found survivors where they reckoned was only 15-- 16,000 men and 17,000 women survived after-- from the whole caboodle after the war, from 480,000 people.
In fact, only 18 months ago, I went to the British Museum where-- where they had a special day for Belsen.
And there were some of the soldiers who actually liberated us, and we had a-- a nice day.
A nice day there.
The memories flooding back, and telling us how by accident they found Belsen.
It was only that one tank somehow got lost on the way that they come across this Belsen-- this camp.
And things were so-- they were telling, and I knew about it because I read in books since.
And I experienced it, mostly.
Things were so bad.
People were dying like flies.

I had to take my string off from my trousers to hold up my trousers, tie me and another guy, or another-- another boy-- tie our string to dead bodies' feet, because they had two wheeled carts and they couldn't cope to drag him about 100, 200 yards, to this great big hole they-- they--
Mass graves.
And throw them in there.
And that was-- everybody was assigned because they tried to hide everything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a terrible thing to do.
By the time you got there, the bit of flesh they had on their body was rubbed away by us dragging them all these-- as I said, I don't know whether it was 100 yards, 200, 300, 500.
I don't remember.
I also learned there was cannibalism in Belsen.
And I myself became very, very, very, very ill.
Either I'm dreaming, or it happened.
I had it-- somebody gave me an injection.
I caught typhus.
I was very ill.
I was there five months.
I was liberated from April the 15th.
I heard a lot of screaming and shouting.
I drag myself out of bed.
I went on all fours.
I crawled to the floor-- to the door.
I looked to the right.
There was nobody on the tower.
No Germans.
I looked on the left.
There was gates wide open.
And people were rushing left, right, and center, screaming.
Rushing with potatoes.
Rushing with bread.
Whatever they could get a hold of.
When I managed to find myself to the kitchens, there was not a scrap to be seen.
Nothing inside.
I finished up sort of wandering along, and I sat on this pile of straw.
And I put my hand down, and I dug down somehow, and I finished up with carrots and onions and [GERMAN].
There was hidden under the straw.
And I sat there and I ate myself sick.
I was sick as a dog.
And I sort of fell asleep there.
I came to, and people were still running around.
I didn't see anybody-- well, I don't know how I found this mountain of vegetables.
I finished up going-- walk-- just wandering around the streets.
And everywhere, the Tommies-- Tommies were standing on street corners.
To go up with this infection.
And they'd infect you and all the rest of it.
Your clothes and whatever.
Because you could just put your hand on your back and pick-- pick lice by the thousands.
There was just thousands.
I finished up once again.
I finished up in a-- this big building.
I went in there, and there was thousands of suits and jackets and trousers.
And I discarded my stripes, and I got into this-- fitted myself out like I was going to a wedding in this lovely suit.
I was so tired, I fell asleep on somebody else's [YIDDISH].
And When I got up, I was-- lice were crawling on me.
And this time, I went voluntarily out, went up to a soldier, and I said, come on.
Do it.
Sort of.
And you know, little things which flash back to-- to you.
And I mean, something like that, you never forget if you live to 120.
And that's-- that's-- that's things would happen.
And you know, gradually people were dying like flies because the German-- the British came in, and they were giving people to conserves.
Corned beef.
Tins of corned beef.
And very fatty foods.
And they were eating one minute and bringing it back-- round the backside the next minute.
They were dying like flies in the thousands.
They were not used to the food and all that.
Me, coming from a little village, I was more interested in a-- in a potato rather than in a-- a tin of spam or whatever.
And by the time they realized what was happening, what they were doing, they stopped it.
They opened kitchens.
They used to issue us with a ticket to line up for dinners.
And they-- we lined up for dinners, and we had like more or less what you get here.
Self-service.
And the girls felt so sorry for us who were dishing out this food that they never even tore our cards up, and we used to eat it, then line up, and have another lot, and so on and so on.
And gradually, you sort of got on your feet, and-- and so on.
And-- and so it went.
So it went.
That was in Belsen.
But it was very, very-- it was-- well, the worst camp I've ever been to.
And I've been to three.
I didn't know what was happening in Auschwitz.
I wasn't long enough there.
In Buchenwald, as I said, I was lucky.
Whereas in Belsen, it was the end of sort of the last four or five months of the war, and they-- it was just terrible.
We used to wait certain times for the-- the dentist and the doctors, but not for hours for the Germans.

They were throwing out peelings of potatoes from the windows above, and we were-- about 20 of us were diving in the mud-- that's how hungry we were-- to pick up-- pick these peelings from potatoes or carrots or whatever it was.
And that is hunger, when people have to dive in mud and things.
And we were lucky to-- that we sort of-- the rest of the people didn't find out, because they would kill you.
But the worst thing which ever-- I'm jumping.
You see, I don't remember-- the worst thing which ever happened to me in Belsen.
One morning, I heard a commotion.
And I went to the door.
And apparently somebody opened one of these barrels of soup, what some people got-- because some people did work for the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How do--
